speaker: interviewer
text: --1995. Interview with Sol Urbach. Tape number 3. You were talking about being in Plaszow when the order came that you could no longer leave the camp for work. Did you go back to work in the quarry at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I wound up again working-- once again working in the quarry. As a matter of fact, on the three occasions that I was there, I-- I somehow wound up on the detail of the quarry. So I don't remember doing any other work other than the quarry.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Schindler, at that time, when he no longer could receive his workers from the concentration camp, he evidently began to scheme on how he could build a concentration camp for himself. And the best we could have-- we could determine at that time was that he did go off to Berlin and receive permission to build his own concentration camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? As, ?] in fact, building a concentration camp can be accomplished if he has a vital industry number 1. And, of course, the vital industry was that the making of the pots and pans could be expanded into building some [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cartridges for armaments, which is similar work as building pots and pans. And lo and behold, the German authorities bought that, that this is such a vital industry that Schindler should be permitted to build his own concentration camp. And he began to build a concentration camp. He eventually got the people-- the 100 people plus some other people from Krakow-Plaszow to help him build this concentration camp adjoining the factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked on some of the barracks at the-- at the-- at the factory-- adjoining the factory. And Schindler would mill around us at that time as-- as we were building this concentration camp. And this concentration camp, to the best of my knowledge, it consisted of either five or six barracks, and it had watchtowers surrounding it, barbed wire. And Schindler was-- which had all the similarities of a-- of a camp like Plaszow. And Schindler would occasionally mill around us and talk about it, how good we will have it in that camp. And at one time, I remember him making a comment that the reason for the barbed wires and the watchtowers in this concentration camp is that not because of fear that we would be escaping from there, but he thinks that we will have it so good in this concentration camp that people from the outside might want to come into the concentration camp. And that's the reason for the barbed wire and the watchtowers. And shortly after the completion of this concentration camp-- this still would have been in 1943, maybe-- maybe summer of 1943 or fall of '43-- we began life in-- in his concentration camp. And the plan had included, by that time, that 1,000 people would work in this concentration camp. So the workforce was changing rapidly. From 100 people, it became 300 and 700. And-- and quickly 1,000 people were in this concentration camp. And this-- our life took on an entirely different picture. It was no longer this fearful, bestial kind of camp that we were in in Krakow-Plaszow. It was the kind of a camp that we worked during the hours of working in the Krakow-- in the Emalia and then would walk out through the gate from the factory into the camp itself. And we-- it was rather peaceful inside the camp. We did not see too many German SS guards coming in. Or if they came in, it was on very rare occasions. Some occasions like that would have been when there was some unloading of trains to be-- to be done, they would come in and round up some people. This was true. But this was so rare that it really was entirely different than the life we had in Krakow-Plaszow. There was food in a better supply. At work, we were in peace. Somehow Schindler was always present there, and we somehow felt that there is a mantle of-- Schindler's mantle protecting us. We were working together at the factory with some non-Jews who would be arriving from the outside of Krakow to work in the factory. And we were the inmates of this camp, but we did work together. I remember working for a fellow who was a-- a carpenter, a cabinet maker. He was my boss inside the Emalia works, and his-- this Polish Christian was a fellow by the name of Wuczek. And I-- I-- from then on, I begin to work as a carpenter, a cabinet maker. I have gained some experience in that field before I even entered the ghetto when I worked for this fellow-- for a fellow by the name of Kaminski in-- before I entered the ghetto. It's the one I described where he was willing to keep me over. And so my work now was as a carpenter in that newly created camp. 1,000 of us lived there. My duties took me to Oskar Schindler's offices because one of my jobs was to maintain the darkout or blackout shades. I would have to see to it that they were in good working order, because when the-- when the airplanes would come over Krakow for bombing missions, these darkout shades would be pulled down. So they always had to be in good working. And I did that, together with other carpentry or cabinet-making work in the shop. Most of the rest of the people worked in the enamel works. And at the same time, Oskar Schindler, according to this plan that he, at one time, delivered to the German authorities, he began to build a new building in which he was going to build these new cartridges for the-- for rockets for the German [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Army industry or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: So from 1943 on, we-- we stayed there. Sometimes in-- in 1944-- and possibly this was already the-- the-- end of-- or no, I'm sorry, the middle of 1944. But before that in-- when I worked in the-- the enamel works factory together with this fellow Wuczek, on occasion this fellow Wuczek would instruct me as a boss. He had the perfect right to instruct me to do whatever he wanted me to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on-- on one of those requests that he had for me is that I steal pots and pans and-- and lower them on a rope to him on the outside of the factory, where he would be able to pick them up at night and take them away into the city of Krakow. I, as a prisoner of that camp itself, I had no benefit from it. There was no-- nothing that I would have gotten paid or received for it. But I simply had to follow the order of my boss. And so I wound up stealing pots and pans from Oskar Schindler's factory for this fellow Wuczek.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he did not need to threaten me. It was an order-- a request from a non-Jew to a Jew inside of a camp was sufficient to get me to do what he wanted to do. The alternative was probably much worse if I didn't listen. And it was easy for me to do that because my movements in the factory itself were a guarantee that I could almost go anyplace because I had to walk to offices. In fact, at one time, I was in charge also of maintaining fires when we were building Schindler's offices. This was during the winter. And there were barrels of fire, and I had to keep feeding them with wood so it would keep the concrete work and the brick work somewhat warm as-- as they were building the offices. Sometimes in the middle of 1944, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the war was turning bad against the Germans. The Russians were advancing westwards. And Schindler was ordered to deliver 700 of the people that he had-- of the 1,000 people that he had there, he was ordered to deliver back to Krakow-Plaszow. And this would have been in the summer of 1944. At that time, I remember standing in formation where Oskar Schindler, together with SS guards and dogs by their sides, were walking up and down the-- the rows of the 1,000 workforce and selecting people that would remain in-- evidently now I know, but at that time I didn't know which group is meant for what. But I remember them doing the selecting. And at one point in time, I realized-- or at least it occurred to me, right or wrong-- it occurred to me that maybe I am in the wrong group of people. And when Schindler came close to where I was standing in that formation, with the SS guards being there, I stepped out of the formation, stepped up to Oskar Schindler, and yelled out to Oskar Schindler, Herr Schindler, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. In English that means, Mr. Schindler, no carpenter was left. At that time, I had absolutely no idea whether what I was saying was true or not true, because I did not know who was selected and who was standing on one group and who was in the other one. But I only-- I said that because that's the only thing that came to my mind, and I just didn't know what else to say to somehow remain with Oskar Schindler. I already knew at that time-- I sensed that already-- that remaining with Schindler whatever will be much better than going anyplace else. Lo and behold, this worked. Oskar Schindler grabbed me by my arm and put me into a group of people that eventually wound up to be a group of 300 people. And these 300 people remained in the concentration camp of Oskar Schindler's, while the other people were delivered in the summer-- end of the summer of 1944, to Krakow-Plaszow. The fate of the other people became known to me after the war. At the time of the war, I did not know that. But after the war, it became known to me as a fact that most of those 700 people that were returned to Krakow-Plaszow wound up in other concentration camps, like Mauthausen, like Dachau, like Auschwitz. As a matter of fact, one of my close buddies at that time that I was friendly with in the concentration camp of Oskar Schindler-- since I had no family, I had to glue on to somebody. So one of those people that I glued onto, became very close with like if we were brothers, was a fellow by the name of Adam Mandel. And Adam Mandel was taken away with the group of 700 and eventually wound up in Mauthausen, and I stayed on at the factory of Oskar Schindler. Following this reduction of the workforce from 1,000 to 300, not long after that a-- a spy plane, a English spy plane or Australian spy plane, flying over Krakow was either shot down or crashed over the city of Krakow. And of all the places, he crashes into our small camp of-- adjoining the factory of the enamel works of Oskar Schindler. And I remember that distinctly. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were extremely fortunate, because he crashed into a barrack that was just vacated by those 700 people that were taken up to Krakow-Plaszow. He crashed into a vacant barrack, into-- and in fact, did not hurt anyone in the group of 300 at all. The plane was full of ammunition. I remember the ammunition popping all night, a fire burning. In the morning, we-- after the fire subsided, we were able to get a little closer. And all I would see is the mountains of-- a mountain of these cartridges from the popped ammunition and a torso of a man still strapped in a seat of this plane. This was an English flyer or an Australian flyer that was burned in that crash. We remained in this concentration camp for a while longer, until we-- until we-- and Schindler's duties by now were to actually pack and-- and be prepared to close up this factory on short notice because the Russians were advancing. And evidently, he had his orders from Amon Goeth from the Krakow-Plaszow concentration camp, under whose supervision evidently he came, that he would have to liquidate. So he was closing up some of the factory buildings. He was preparing some of the machinery for loading and shipment. But of course, at that time, we had no idea what his plans were. But Schindler, at this point in time, in the summer of 1944, while it was not known to me, maybe it was known to some other people, that Schindler, in fact, did not give up on us, was not willing to surrender us altogether to the fate that would meet us if we were-- if he did not try to protect us. And he evidently began to scheme a new plan that he would-- this industry is so important-- and he obviously convinced the authorities again-- that his industry is important enough to move all his equipment and all his skilled labor to a concentration camp that he would create deep in Czechoslovakia. And he needed, obviously, lots of permission to do that. But after sometimes in October of 1944, when we were ordered-- the 300 of-- or let's put it this way-- when Schindler was ordered to deliver the 300 of us back to Krakow-Plaszow one more time, he already had hatched a-- hatched a plan to build a new factory and a new concentration camp deep in Czechoslovakia. How this would play out with-- with us in the-- in Krakow-Plaszow, we had no idea. Nobody spoke to us. Nobody told us that there is any kind of a list. Nobody told us where we are going. But in October of 1944, we arrived in Krakow-Plaszow. And within days or a week or two, we were loaded on cattle cars in Krakow-Plaszow. The haste was going on at that time in Krakow-Plaszow-- many hangings going on, many shootings going on, killings, brutalities. It was a camp in haste to close. Evidently, Amon Goeth had his orders to liquidate and kill as many people as he can in order to close down that camp because the Russians were advancing rapidly at this time. In October of 1944 this was. Unbeknown to me, the 300 of us that were remnants in Oskar Schindler's factory, the 300 of those people, together with 400 others and the-- and the women, and the 300 women, were loaded into cattle cars for shipment someplace, to somewhere. The men, the 700 men, were loaded up in Krakow-Plaszow. And while we didn't know our destination at all, and we knew nothing about Oskar Schindler-- if there were some people in our group that knew about Oskar Schindler, there-- they knew it. I did not. So we were loaded up into cattle cars, and the women were shipped to Auschwitz. The men were shipped to Gross-Rosen. In October of 1944, we arrived in Gross-Rosen. It was extremely cold already at that time. When we arrived in Gross-Rosen, the-- we were greeted there by-- by brutal SS guards. Most of those SS guards in the Krakow-- in the Gross-Rosen camp were volunteers-- Lithuanians, Ukrainians, Poles, and extremely brutal people. When we arrived there, they quickly ordered us into formations. There were latrines there that we finally could relieve ourselves of because we were packed into the cattle cars for all this time. And we--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did the trip take?

speaker: interviewee
text: The trip took someplace-- 7, 10 days. I really would have absolutely no 100% knowledge of what it was. It was many days, many shipments. The rail-- the cattle cars were shifted from siding to siding because of the-- the trip could have been very short. But there were transports of-- of army, German Army, flowing through the tracks, and therefore we had to be sidetracked on occasion. So we probably did not make good time in getting to Gross-Rosen. The distance couldn't have been that great, but it took us many, many days. In Gross-Rosen, we were standing on the Appellplatz, stripped totally, no clothes, were ordered to run towards the showers, toward the delousing facility. We entered the showers. We-- we needed to be clean for whatever would happen. In the shower-- after we were showered, we were given some piece of clothing, just like one piece of clothing, whatever it happened to be. It was either a shirt or a jacket or-- or pants. This is what you got, you got. And that was your-- your clothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I remember getting some kind of a gown, which was long, and this was my clothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] If I ever got a-- a-- another striped outfit, I don't recall that, whether there was enough time for me to get that or not. I don't recall it. I somehow recall that I wound up on the transport with just that one piece of clothing that I had. And there we were in Gross-Rosen itself. Once we came out of the showers, we faced a battery of-- of guards who were giving us a crew cut. After the crew cut, they-- the guards attempted to shave our-- shave a 2-inch strip from front to back on the head. And evidently, this was designed so to keep us from being able to flee from the concentration camp. As the guards were shaving that 2-inch inch strip from front to back, they would spit on our heads to make that shaving process possible. Sometimes before, while we were still at Oskar Schindler's factory, we did receive a tattoo. The tattoo was not a number, but it was a big KL. It was a KL, K standing for [? Konzentrazionslager-- ?] L for Lager, K for [? Konzentrazions. ?] This was given to us yet in Schindler's camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Emalia, under the orders of, evidently, the-- Amon Geoth. This was given to us to prevent us from escape. The-- at that time, and feeling the-- a little safe under Schindler's mantle, a rumor had it that if you sucked out the tattoo right immediately after it was done, that could disappear, and then you would still have a chance to escape.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, actually, I was among that group of people that sucked out the tattoo. I don't know where-- why I wasn't full of fear in doing it, but I did do it. I sucked it out. And lo and behold, today I don't have a tattoo on my arm of KL.

speaker: interviewee
text: And yet there are friends that were together with me and eventually were liberated together with me and survived the war, and they have a inch-and-a-half tattoo on their arm with the KL. But when I got to Gross-Rosen, I no longer had a KL. But when we arrived in Gross-Rosen, we stayed all in a-- this group of 700, as I recall, stayed in one barrack. We were separated from the rest of the population of that concentration camp. We were kept separate. And we had no knowledge on why we are being kept separate and what is to happen to us eventually. But for the next few days, we did not do much else than go out to the back yard for counting and recounting of us and-- and occasional beatings of-- of us. The SS guards there had a nasty habit of kick-- kicking people, kicking people until a person would fall to the ground. And when that person looked unconscious or unhappy enough, the SS guards would leave him alone again. But this was their-- their habit in that concentration camp. We stayed in this one-- in this one barrack. And if I recall it, the center of that barrack had a very nice, clean room that looked like a kitchen with pots and pans hanging in there. And it looked like a-- a respectable kind of a place. But I-- I know now what this was for, and this was for the ready inspections. In case the Red Cross or somebody would come in to a camp, they would take them through this-- through this center room, and they would see how orderly the prisoners are kept in those rooms, and evidently food supply was there. But our food was-- once a day, we received some kind of a thin soup of something. Whatever was in it, we don't know. But I know that we were waken up at the-- at a godly hour of 4 o'clock when it was extremely dark yet. This is October night, very dark. We would be waken up to go to the mess hall and receive this soup, and-- and they divvy it up. And I remember them dishing out that soup in that camp. And they were dishing it out in such a manner, in such a fast manner, that if you walked up and you didn't have your-- your canteen right there at the right spot, you miss the soup, and you would not get it anymore. And so this was a kind of an extremely brutal concentration camp. And-- and the worst part was that we had no idea on where-- what is happening, what will be happening to us. It was only after a few days stay that, actually, a-- a SS guard came into our-- our barrack. And the-- and the situation was so bad and crowded in this barrack that all we had for space for ourselves to exist in this barrack is to-- was to simply sit on the floor in one another's lap. That's how we stayed for hours and days, in one another's lap, because that's all the space that was [? allowed ?] for us in the barrack.
